title: Method Overriding
subtitle: Customise inherited behaviour in child classes
learning_objectives: Override a parent method in a child class
Use super().method() to extend (not replace) parent behaviour
Understand polymorphism — same method name, different behaviour
Call the correct method when working with a list of mixed objects

sections.heading: Overriding Methods
sections.content: If a child class defines a method with the same name as a parent method, the child's version replaces (overrides) the parent's version. Python always calls the most specific version.

sections.heading: Using super() to Extend Behaviour
sections.content: Instead of completely replacing the parent method, you can call super().method() to run the parent's version first, then add extra behaviour.
sections.code_output:
I am Rex.
I am a Labrador.

sections.heading: Polymorphism
sections.content: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. When different classes implement the same method name, you can call that method on any object without caring which specific class it is. This makes code much more flexible.

exercises.title: Override a Method
exercises.prompt: Create a Shape class with area() that returns 0. Create a Circle class inheriting Shape with __init__(radius) and override area() to return 3.14159 * radius * radius. Print Circle(5).area().
hints: Circle(5).area() creates a circle of radius 5 and immediately calls area()

exercises.title: Polymorphism in Action
exercises.prompt: Create Dog and Cat classes each with a speak() method returning 'Woof!' and 'Meow!' respectively. Put them in a list and print the result of calling speak() on each.
hints: The starter code is complete — just run it!
Each class has speak() — Python calls the right version based on the object type

quiz.question: If a child class defines a method with the same name as the parent, what happens when you call it?
options: Both the parent and child versions run
The parent version runs (parent takes priority)
The child version runs (child overrides the parent)
quiz.explanation: The child class's version overrides the parent's. Python always uses the most specific version available. To also run the parent version, you can call super().method_name() inside the child's method.

quiz.question: What is polymorphism in OOP?
options: When a class has many attributes
When the same method name works differently on different objects
When a function has default parameters
quiz.explanation: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. Different classes can implement the same method name, and the correct version is called automatically based on the object's type. This allows you to write flexible code that works with many different types of objects.

completion_message: Excellent! You understand method overriding and polymorphism — key OOP concepts!
